data.title: Nova: A New Star In The Making
data.description:
In September, I adopted a measured approach to shares of Nova Measurements (NASDAQ:NVMI) in which I praised the business for quadrupling sales over the past decade, accompanied by huge margin gains.

A mere $2 stock in the early 2000s was still trading at just $20 in 2019, after which huge momentum unleashed the stock to a high of $250 over the summer. Momentum has continued over the past half a year, rightfully sending shares continuously higher.

I greatly admire the business here, but am not willing to commit given the recent momentum, although I am very eager to keep a close eye on the shares.

An Advanced Semiconductor Player

Nova is an innovator and provider of dimensional materials and chemical metrology solutions, used in advanced process control within semiconductor manufacturing. These solutions are based on advanced technologies relating to optical scatterometry, x-ray fluorescence and photoelectron spectroscopy, among others.

The smaller chips get, the more process nodes are required, with capital investments per wafer exponentially on the increase as well. This is also driven by adoption of new technologies such as spectral interferometry, generative AI, raman spectroscopy and direct metal replenishment, among others.

These solutions have been key in advancing the goals of its semiconductor clients, as the company has quadrupled sales to over half a billion in just a decade's time, while operating margins tripled to about 30% of sales.

2023 was somewhat of a challenging year, as was the case for many, with sales reported down 9% to $518 million, with operating earnings down 12% to $132 million. This year of stagnation, however, followed a spectacular 37% increase in 2022 sales. GAAP earnings came in at $136 million, resulting in earning of just over $4 per share based on a share tally of 32 million shares.

Momentum Returns

In May, Nova reported a 6% increase in first quarter sales to $142 million, as momentum improved during the year. Second quarter sales rose as much as 28% to $157 million, while record bookings were reported, across geographies, customer accounts and technologies.

On this quarterly revenue number, GAAP earnings were reported at $45 million, equal to $1.41 per share. While this was impressive, it included $8 million in net interest income, equal to $0.27 per share. Adjusted for this, realistic earnings were trending at $4 and change per share.

Trading at $200 over the summer, operating assets were valued around $5.6 billion, equal to about 10 times sales given after factoring in substantial net cash holdings. Trading at 40 times realistic earnings, valuations were demanding, but this was backed up by real operating momentum.

This came after the company guided for third quarter sales between $168 and $176 million. Believing that the business traded around 8 times annualised sales and 35 times earnings (based on the third quarter guidance) I was aware of the track record and momentum, yet feared a dangerous set-up in case momentum would fade.

On Fire - Again

After shares declined during the fall, and traded around the $170 mark in November, shares have seen great momentum so far in 2025. After a relentless rally, shares trade at $268 per share, and that is even after shares are down twenty dollars from the highs.

The fall in November coincided with a 39% increase in reported third quarter sales, with revenues of $179 million coming in comfortably above the guided range. Moreover, momentum was set to continue, with fourth quarter sales seen between $181 and $191 million.

Later that month, Nova announced a $60 million bolt-on deal to acquire German-based Sentronics, a wafer metrology tools provider used for backend semiconductor manufacturing. This was really a bolt-on deal, with few details announced, other than that the deal was anticipated to be accretive to non-GAAP earnings per share upon closing.

By mid-February, Nova reported a 45% increase in fourth quarter sales to $195 million, again outpacing the guided range. Operating profits of $55 million could come in at $44 million (excluding net interest income), for earnings of $1.37 per share based on a share tally of 32 million shares (on a diluted basis). At this pace, earnings are trending close to $5.50 per share, that is excluding net interest income.

Growth Continues

While the company ended the year on a spectacular note, momentum is only set to continue into 2025. First quarter sales are seen between $205 and $215 million, with further advancements seen on the bottom line as well. This makes it very easy to see earnings trend at $6-7 per share (excluding interest income).

These cash holdings are very substantial at $811 million and will remain at three-quarter of a billion following the purchase of Sentronics, equal to $23 per share based on the diluted share count. This implies that operating assets trade at $245 per share, equal to 35-40 times earnings, based on earnings power of $6-7 per share.

While I am very impressed with the strong performance, I am mindful of 60% returns seen in just about three months time, as a $170 level (with the benefit of hindsight) looked like a great bargain in November.

At current levels I am erring to the cautious side, as I judge the situation to be somewhat similar to September, with current earnings multiples still being demanding after a period of momentum seen already. That said, the great growth trajectory and somewhat reasonable multiples, make that Nova could become an acquisition target as well in my view at some point in time.

At current levels I am very impressed by the business, yet mindful of the momentum seen already as well, making me take a wait-and-see-approach. This also comes in part as China is responsible for about 40% of sales, and the wider Asian region combined making up about 80% of sales.

I look forward with great interest to learn more on the business momentum in 2025, and to turn more upbeat if unexpected hiccups might be seen in the stock.

data.title: Norfolk Southern: From Setback To Comeback
data.description:
Introduction

Norfolk Southern (NYSE:NSC) embodies a recovery play that has recently outperformed the market. After the derailment in East Palestine, OH, the company faced significant costs that pressured its margins and tanked the stock. But, last July, I argued it could possibly be the best railroad investment in the short term. As a matter of fact, it beat all of its peers. The reason was foreseeable, alongside increasing intermodal traffic and rising demand for chemicals (one of the largest commodities NSC moves), the company was starting to report earnings against very easy comparables and was getting back on track to run its operations smoothly.

Well, as some may know, eight months ago, I happened to be a net seller of stocks as I was raising some cash for other needs. As a result, I didn't buy Norfolk Southern, and I still don't own it. In fact, right now, I have 5% of my portfolio concentrated in Canadian National (CNI), I don't own Union Pacific (UNP) anymore, and I only have a tiny fraction of my portfolio invested in Canadian Pacific (CP). This doesn't mean I am not bullish on the industry overall or that I am not interested in Norfolk Southern. Hence, it is about time to update my research after the idea that the stock would have offered a decent upside in the second half of 2024 played out. We have to answer two questions:

  1. How is Norfolk performing in a mixed environment?
  2. Has Norfolk caught up with its fair value, or is there still some upside left?

So, without further ado, let's take a look at the whole picture, starting with the latest earnings report and then collecting some data from the macroeconomic environment to understand what Norfolk's Q1 may look like.

Norfolk Southern's Q4 Earnings Review

Norfolk Southern reported revenues of $3B, which represented a 2% YoY decrease. The operating ratio in Q4 was 62.6%, which shows further improvement as the company strives to get the ratio back to below 60%. While in 2023, the Ohio incident brought over $1.1B in extra costs, this year, total incident-related expenses were down to $325M, significantly helping the company's margins and profits. As a result, Norfolk's operating expenses improved by 7%, and the operating margin decreased by 390bps YoY. In Q4, the reported operating income of $1.1B was 11% above Q4 2023. This proves that Norfolk was indeed going against easy comparables.

For the full year, Norfolk reported flat top-line growth, a 3% decrease in opex, a 160bps operating ratio improvement, a 5% growth in operating income, and an 11% growth in net income. A big help came from achieving nearly $300M in cost savings, beating the target of $250M.

Norfolk guided for 3% revenue growth and a 150bps OR improvement. It also announced it will resume its share repurchases starting in Q1 2025. Since early 2023, Norfolk has paid a steady $1.35 dividend to protect its balance sheet before the huge costs of the East Palestine incident. A few weeks ago, Norfolk declared another $1.35 quarterly dividend, but the path to delever the balance sheet has been taken, and we could then see a dividend hike in the second half of the year. But the fact that Norfolk is resuming its share repurchases restores part of its value creation framework. This is critical because its main competitor, CSX Corporation (CSX), has been able to keep reducing its outstanding shares and created a gap of almost 5pps in just less than two years.

Norfolk reports its freight revenues under three categories: merchandise, intermodal, and coal. This last one is the most important one for Norfolk since its rail network unfolds through the Appalachians, where coal is mined. While coals bears bad news and is expected to be down this year to due to lower natural gas prices, intermodal is expected to keep growing. Merchandise shows a mixed picture, with grain and chemicals performing well, while automotive and metals should be down vs. last year.

Does this outlook find support anywhere? Let's take a look.

Railroading in 2025

As the Association of American Railroads explains, "strong consumer demand drives strong intermodal movements," but "tariffs are at the forefront" of the public debate. This year, freight rail is slightly up YoY, with a +0.2% increase in January. The carloads mix shows coal (the largest carload commodity by volume) continues its decline, while grain and chemicals surged. Industrial products, which include cars but also crushed stone and gravel, are declining, reflecting a still soft industrial economy in the U.S.

Nonetheless, when chemicals perform well, we can expect an industrial rebound to take place soon. As a matter of fact, the ISM manufacturing PMI rose to 50.9%, finally crossing the critical threshold of 50%, which separates recession from expansion. This suggests cautious optimism for this year, although manufacturing rebounds are not quick processes, especially when interest rates are high, and many still expect a few cuts soon.

Norfolk has to release a weekly traffic report. If we look at the last one (week 7 ending February 9, 2025), we see that, so far this year, chemicals and grain are indeed growing YoY. But total merchandise carloads are actually down by over 10k loadings. However, intermodal is truly doing great, and it is helping the whole company report a total increase in carloadings. However, intermodal also has a lower revenue-per-carload metric. So, this mix might be a bit detrimental to Norfolk's top line.

However, from what we are seeing reported until now, the goal of 3% revenue growth seems to be reached by the end of the year. Some revenue growth will be helpful while the company improves its efficiency: it means more profits are coming in, and this will help Norfolk further protect its balance sheet.

Let's speak a bit about the threat posed by tariffs. Surely, intermodal will be hurt. But, in any case, whether goods are produced within the U.S. or outside, they will still need to be transported. As a result, I am not that concerned about this threat.

Valuation

We answered the first question. Now we tackle the second one: is Norfolk fairly valued or not? In a mixed environment, it is difficult to forecast explosive growth for the next few years. After all, this is not what investing in Class 1 railroads is about. This is a playground for LT-oriented investors who are willing to let these stocks compound while collecting growing dividends.

Right now, Norfolk trades at a fwd PE just below 20, which means it is less expensive than the market. Considering 2025 will be a year of EPS growth, the fwd PE ratio is currently graded with a B- by Seeking Alpha. The P/FCF, a very important ratio for railroads, is 15, but we also have to consider that Norfolk's capex will come a bit down in future years after it ends paying for the incident in Ohio. In 2026, analysts expect Norfolk's EPS to be $14.92, meaning the fwd PE is 17. More or less, the market expects Norfolk to keep growing between 3% to 6% per year while improving its efficiency and, thus, its EPS. If buybacks resume, too, it would not be absurd to expect Norfolk to grow its EPS above 10% per year. In this case, the PE ratio will be reduced by 1.5/2 points per year. Moreover, the dividend yield is now above 2%, and this is high for Norfolk's average.

In a market where almost everything is expensive, I think Norfolk still offers a decent opportunity, and this is why I keep rating it as a buy.

Index

The gross expense ratio is the fund’s total annual operating costs, expressed as a percentage of the fund’s average net assets for a given time period. It is gross of any fee waivers or expense reimbursement. The net expense ratio is the expense ratio after the application of any waivers or reimbursement. This is the actual ratio that investors paid during the fund’s most recent fiscal year. Please see the prospectus for more information.

Returns or yields for the fund would have be lower if a portion of the management fee had not been waived. Review the semiannual or annual report for the most current information. Expense ratio is as of the fund's current prospectus. The I Class minimum investment amount is$5 million($3 million for endowments and foundations) per fund.

Periods greater than one year have been annualized.

Market Review

Munis retreated. Alongside rising Treasury yields and persistent inflation, U.S. bonds broadly declined in the quarter. However, municipal bonds(munis) generally fared better than the taxable fixed-income market. A third-quarter rally, aided by the September launch of the Federal Reserve’s easing campaign, helped most bond sectors, including munis, maintain one-year gains.

Economy remained resilient. The U.S. economy grew 3.1%(annualized) in the third quarter, similar to the second quarter. Other data released in the fourth quarter were generally upbeat. Led by services sector strength, activity in the private sector expanded, while consumer confidence improved, and retail sales increased. However, manufacturing continued to contract, and the job market weakened.

Munis outperformed Treasuries. Muni yields tracked Treasury yields higher during the quarter, but at a slower pace, and most muni-to-Treasury ratios rose. Overall, munis outperformed Treasuries. Additionally, general obligation(GO) bonds fared better than revenue bonds, and shorter-maturity bonds generally outperformed longer-maturity issues.

Supply slowed in the quarter, grew for the year. As expected, fourth-quarter municipal bond supply shrunk compared with the third quarter’s level, as many issuers pushed deals forward to avoid presidential election-triggered volatility. For the year, total muni supply increased 29% compared with 2023, according to Barclays.

Fundamentals remained stable. While municipal credit trends continued to level off, balance sheets for most state and local entities remained fairly robust. Fiscal 2025 budgets for most U.S. states include modest revenue growth and conservative spending plans alongside strong reserve balances and liquidity. Additionally, rating upgrades slightly outpaced downgrades, and defaults remained rare.

High-yield munis outperformed. Muni credit spreads tightened modestly for the quarter. High-yield municipal bonds declined but outperformed investment-grade munis for the quarter. For the year, high-yield munis significantly outperformed their investment-grade peers.

Portfolio Performance Review

Duration detracted. Our longer-than-index duration position weighed on the portfolio’s performance compared with the benchmark as yields rose. Our positioning along the yield curve, which favored short and intermediate maturities, helped offset some negative duration effects.

Security selection contributed. Security selection boosted results compared with the benchmark, largely due to our choices in the hospital, water and sewer and corporate muni sectors. These choices more than offset negative selection effects in the retirement community, special tax and multifamily housing sectors.

Sector allocations helped. Underweight positions relative to the benchmark in the prerefunded, other utility, state GO bond and multifamily housing sectors lifted results compared with the benchmark. These weightings more than offset the negative effects from overweight positions in the corporate muni and tobacco sectors and an underweight in local GO bonds. Additionally, our high-yield overweight modestly aided results.

Positioning for the Future

Growth may gather momentum. We expect slower growth in early 2025, as Trump administration initiatives, including new tariffs and government efficiency efforts, may weaken consumer demand and employment. By 2025’s second half, deregulation, onshoring and rekindled animal spirits may reignite growth. As the Trump administration's tax policy unfolds, we’ll monitor the potential muni market effects.

Credit backdrop to moderate. We expect previously strong credit trends to continue moderating. However, healthy reserves and sound liquidity should help mitigate the effects of slowing revenue growth and waning federal stimulus funds. We also expect an increase in state and local governments facing larger projected budget gaps, such as those challenging Chicago and Oakland, California.

Focused on quality. Given tight valuations and our expectations for growth to slow, we are maintaining a bias toward higher-quality securities and a slight duration overweight relative to the benchmark. As always, stringent security selection guides our process.

Valuations and fiscal policy uncertainties are keeping us focused on select sectors, waiting for better opportunities to boost credit exposure.

Data presented reflects past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance shown. To obtain performance data current to the most recent month end, please visit www.americancentury.com/performance. Investment return and share value will fluctuate, and redemption value may be more or less than original cost. Data assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. For information about other share classes available, please consult the prospectus. There is no guarantee that the investment objectives will be met. Dividends and yields represent past performance and there is no guarantee that they will continue to be paid.

You should consider the fund’s investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses carefully before you invest. The fund’s prospectus or summary prospectus, which can be obtained at American Century Investments® Home, contains this and other information about the fund, and should be read carefully before investing.

The opinions expressed are those of the portfolio investment team and are no guarantee of the future performance of any American Century Investments portfolio. Statements regarding specific holdings represent personal views and compensation has not been received in connection with such views. This information is for an educational purpose only and is not intended to serve as investment advice.

The information is not intended as a personalized recommendation or fiduciary advice and should not be relied upon for investment, accounting, legal or tax advice.

Interest rate changes are among the most significant factors affecting bond return. Generally, as interest rates rise, the value of the securities held in the fund will decline. The opposite is true when interest rates decline.

The letter ratings indicate the credit worthiness of the underlying bonds in the portfolio and generally range from AAA(highest) to D(lowest).

The lower-rated securities in which the fund invests are subject to greater default and liquidity risk, because the issuers of high-yield securities are more sensitive to real or perceived economic changes.

There is no guarantee that the investment objectives will be met. Fund shares are not guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Investment income may be subject to certain state and local taxes and, depending on your tax status, the federal alternative minimum tax. Capital gains are not exempt from federal income tax.

The S&P Municipal Bond 50% Investment Grade/50% High Yield Index consists of bonds from the S&P Municipal Bond Investment Grade Index& S&P Municipal Bond High Yield ex Puerto Rico Index. Commentary Glossary

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities(Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax(AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities(ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points(BPs): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for“carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations(CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations(CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities(CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index(CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration(CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or“cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest-paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures(usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency

overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets(EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund(ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee(FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve(Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts(Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation(GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product(GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises(GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield(HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade(IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard& Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A“market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships(MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher-yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI(All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of

U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into"pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures(PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing(QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts(REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool.

ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later(to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities(maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality(credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard“swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns(or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities(TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct"full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills(maturing in one year or less), notes(maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds(maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes(VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic(daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any"inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Glossary Sources: American Century Investments, Bloomberg Indices.

American Century Investments Services, Inc., Distributor©2025 American Century Proprietary Holdings, Inc. All rights reserved.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CME Group: High Quality With Good Dividend Yield
data.description:
CME Group (NASDAQ:CME) runs a global marketplace for trading futures and options, and is the dominant such company. For such an old legacy company, CME has only been public since 2002. It had a mutually owned model from inception to this point. Below are the returns since IPO:

Next are the return metrics versus peers.

Company

Revenue 10-Year CAGR

Median 10-Year ROE

Median 10-Year ROIC

EPS 10-Year CAGR

FCF 10-Year CAGR

CME

0.6%

26.7%

14.6%

5.6%

11.3%

NDAQ

-4.7%

-2.1%

-0.6%

-1.9%

19.1%

CBOE

-0.9%

14.6%

7.4%

3.5%

n/a

ICE

0.6%

43.2%

19.2%

10.8%

11.8%

The nature of their business model creates a near-monopoly. Traders clearly need liquidity above all else, so the biggest player in town will continue to attract the trading activity. It is important to note the difficulty of anyone, even a disruptive fintech company, to meaningfully compete is immense. This meets the definition of a quality company, and their FCF margins are extraordinary at around 60% currently. This is a capital-light business model, yet comes with huge regulatory costs and requirements at the same time.

Capital Allocation

Below we see how capital was allocated over the past decade:

Year

2014

2015

2016

2017

2018

2019

2020

2021

2022

2023

Revenue

3,113

3,327

3,595

3,645

4,309

4,868`

4,884

4,690

5,019

5,579

EBIT

1,768

1,989

2,201

2,311

2,608

2,588

2,637

2,645

3,016

3,436

FCF

1,151

1,148

1,640

1,669

2,324

2,427

2,518

2,275

2,966

3,377

Dividends

1,497

1,343

1,787

1,994

2,150

1,696

2,110

2,189

2,634

3,236

Acquisitions

262

90

21

145

96

Debt Repayment

750

673

452

662

305

756

16

SBC

55

61

66

58

96

73

96

75

84

83

While clearly a mature business, their acquisitions since being a public company have been absolutely crucial to their dominant market share today. Looking at how their major acquisitions helped form the current edition of this company, it's surprising in the context of today’s antitrust activity, that they were able to acquire their biggest competitors essentially. From 2007-2012, they acquired the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT), New York Mercantile Exchange (NYMEX) and the Kansas City Board of Trade (KCBT).

They have always paid out a significant amount in dividends, but the days of big acquisitions are mostly over at this point. I would prefer a shift towards repurchases over dividends at this point (but still both), but it doesn’t seem likely. The focus will continue to be on dividends and smaller acquisitions.

Risk

I consider myself a highly qualitative investor, and among these circles there is a huge emphasis on ROE and more importantly ROIC. My favorite examples of this would be Mastercard (MA) or VeriSign (VRSN). Their NOPAT increases over time, while invested capital grows minimally. This isn't exactly the case with CME.

Their margins are nothing short of excellent. You might notice that ROE and ROIC are not correspondingly as high, but there are some explanations for this. One is the highly regulated nature of CME’s main lines of business, which adds operational costs, thus driving down NOPAT. The second reason is that as a clearinghouse, CME is required to hold large sums of capital to make sure that trades are backed and the market has stability. So all of this regulatory capital falls under “invested capital”, but it isn’t a true capital investment in the traditional sense. This regulatory capital doesn’t actually increase operating profits. What it does is, give CME something of a “regulatory moat.”

My overall point is that this is one of those rare, quality businesses with huge structural advantages. Just because the ROIC isn’t as high as other notable quality companies, it doesn’t mean it isn’t also of high quality. Aside from the above-mentioned regulatory capital, CME’s business requires CAPEX of around 3% of revenue or less. The common feature of a company of this quality is that the biggest risk is mostly in ignoring valuation.

Earnings

Let's take a look at recent earnings history:

As a longer term investor, I look at quarterly earnings mostly as opportunities to buy the dip of something on my qualitative watchlist. Just like any trade, these buy the dip moments are much more clear in hindsight. This is evident when looking at the 5-year stock chart:

Regardless of the past, CME is a stock that is probably worth averaging UP on instead of just down. More on that next.

Valuation

Shares reached an all-time high at the previous earnings announcement recently. Correspondingly, their TTM revenue, operating income, and FCF are all at record highs as well. First, let's compare the stock multiples to peers and on a historical basis:

Company

EV/Sales

EV/EBITDA

EV/FCF

P/B

Div. Yield

CME

14.5

20.9

24.1

3.1

4.4%

NDAQ

7.3

21.2

38.2

4.1

1.1%

ICE

9.5

18.8

29

3.5

1.1%

CBOE

5.5

7.2

14.4

5.2

1.2%

In this case, multiples aren’t extremely helpful. CME has such dominant market share that it deserves a higher multiple in general. The rising multiples over time also make sense because the acquisitions successfully improved the company rather than simply growing, but also by eliminating most competition.

So now lets move to the DCF model:

I see the stock as slightly undervalued now, but keep in mind that it is still primarily a dividend stock. Historically, they’ve paid out all or most of FCF as dividends.

For a company that returns so much to shareholders in the form of dividends, you should be aiming for a dividend yield higher than the index average. This hasn’t been the case for many blue-chip stocks over the years. Many blue-chip stocks have had dividend yields stuck at 2% or less, while the PE is over 25 at the same time.

I would by no means call this a "back the truck up" moment, but I do think CME is slightly overvalued here, and the 4% yield is enough to warrant holding one of the highest quality business models in the world.

Conclusion

CME is a true legacy company, and fits the definition of a high-quality business, a category that necessarily has a limited number. I’m bullish on the company and like the share price today, but keep in mind this is still a dividend stock. Despite the incredible business model, dividends will likely make up most of your future return at this point. The downside risk on a fundamental level is extremely low, and capital allocation plays a huge role in producing shareholder returns. While I would much rather prefer to see repurchases take place over dividends, plenty of capital is being returned to shareholders, and this should only continue.

data.title: TLTW: DOGE Cuts May Benefit Bonds
data.description:
It's been over a year since I last covered the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (BATS:TLTW). In my last article, I was cautious about the fund, as I did not like TLTW's capped upside/uncapped downside returns profile and the worsening outlook for America's fiscal situation. Since my article, the TLTW ETF has lost 9.0% in value compared to a 4.0% decline for the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), partly justifying my caution (Figure 1).

Fortunately, the TLTW ETF also earned ample option premium income in the past year to bring its total returns to a modest 4.9%. In fact, the TLTW has outperformed the TLT, which has delivered 0.0% in total returns since my February 2024 article, as long-term treasury yields have gone essentially sideways for the past 2.5 years (Figure 2).

With President Trump recently returning to the White House and Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency ("DOGE") actively dismantling government agencies and budgets, has the U.S. government's fiscal outlook improved materially such that the TLTW ETF is now worth a buy?

In my opinion, DOGE's budget cuts may spur a slowdown in U.S. economic growth, which could lead to lower long-term bond yields. This should be supportive for long-term treasury bonds and the TLTW ETF. However, if yields fall rapidly, the TLTW's price gains may be capped by its sold call options. For investors looking for lower bond yields, the TLTW is not the best bet.

Furthermore, black swan risks are also rising, as some of Trump's proposals may lead to a surge in treasury yields if global investors lose faith in American institutions and values.

Weighing the risks and total returns potential, I continue to rate the TLTW ETF a hold.

Brief Fund Overview

First, for those not familiar, the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond BuyWrite Strategy ETF ("TLTW") is one of a wave of recently launched 'derivative income funds' that combine an option-writing strategy (most often writing call options ) with traditional vanilla investment funds or themes. The TLTW ETF tracks the investment returns of holding the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) and writing one-month covered call options to generate income.

Since launching in August 2022, the TLTW has been quite successful in attracting assets, with the fund's total assets under management ("AUM") now over $1 billion (Figure 3). The TLTW ETF charges investors a 0.35% expense ratio.

Losing Principal Is A Feature, Not A Bug

As I mentioned at the beginning of this article, the TLTW ETF has lost 9.0% in principal since my last article. In fact, since its launch in August 2022, the TLTW ETF has lost more than 40.0% of its principal (i.e. market value) compared to a 21.1% decline in the TLT ETF (Figure 4). What explains this destruction of investors' principal?

The main issue for the TLTW ETF is its' strategy. As I have warned in numerous prior articles, covered-call funds like the TLTW have a major flaw in that their sold call options cap the funds' upside returns. In TLTW's case, the ETF writes monthly call options that are 2% out-of-the-money ("OTM"). If the TLT ETF rallies more than 2% in a given month, the TLTW's returns are 'capped' at 2%, as the fund has traded away returns beyond 2% for upfront premium income.

However, because the TLT ETF is a bet on long-term interest rates, its monthly returns are quite volatile and can routinely swing from -13% to 15% on a month to month basis (Figure 5). Overall, approximately 30% of TLT's monthly returns are greater than 2%, so the TLTW ETF will give up returns beyond 2% in those months.

At the same time, the TLTW retains most of the downside of owning long-term treasury bonds. Therefore, the TLTW ETF's monthly returns distribution is 'left skewed', with principal declines more likely than gains. This 'skewed distribution' explains why TLTW ETF's 'principal' has shrunk by over 40% since its inception.

The offset for the TLTW ETF is that the fund also earns option premium income from selling call options. Including the TLTW ETF's distribution yield, the TLTW ETF has returned -10.3% in total returns since inception compared to -13.7% for the TLT ETF (Figure 6).

TLTW May Be More Tax Efficient

One aspect of the TLTW ETF I have not discussed previously is its tax efficiency. On an after-tax basis, the TLTW ETF may have a hidden tax benefit that is underappreciated. For example, in 2024, the TLTW ETF paid $3.404493 / share in distributions, with $3.063455 (or 90%) of the distribution considered return-of-capital ("ROC"). In contrast, the TLT ETF's $3.754910 / share distribution is 100% considered ordinary dividends.

Dollar-for-dollar, TLTW's distribution may be more tax-efficient than TLT's. So the after-tax total returns gap between TLTW and TLT may be even wider than the numbers quoted in Figure 6 above. However, as I am not a tax expert, I recommend investors interested in this topic to consult a tax professional.

DOGE Vows To Cut Government Waste

One of President Trump's campaign promises was to cut government waste and reduce the budget deficit. To that end, President Trump has entrusted Elon Musk with control of the Department of Government Efficiency ("DOGE") and a goal to cut $2 trillion over 10 years from the federal budget.

From the day of Trump's inauguration, Mr. Musk and his team at DOGE have been on a destructive rampage, dismantling government agencies like the United States Agency for International Development (USAID) and recommending the government cull tens of thousands of its workforce.

So far, Mr. Musk claims to have eliminated $49 billion in government waste, which is approximately 2.5% of his goal (Figure 7). However, there is also criticism from pundits and analysts that Mr. Musk's DOGE cuts appear to be politically motivated.

Reduce Benefits To Pay For Tax Cuts?

Putting aside the politics of DOGE's budget cuts, even if Musk is successful in identifying and cutting $2 trillion from the federal budget, the savings may not go toward reducing the national debt, which currently stands at over $35 trillion.

Instead, the federal budget savings will be applied to 'pay for' an extension of Trump's Tax Cut And Jobs Act, which is expected to cost $4.5 trillion. In effect, President Trump and Elon Musk may be simply cutting from areas like foreign aid, military, education, and healthcare in order to pay for their proposed tax cuts.

Does The US Really Have A Spending Problem?

Taking a step back, one question I have is whether the U.S. government truly has a spending problem. Since I posed this question in my prior article, I did more research and the results are not as clear-cut as I had imagined.

Readers may recognize Figure 8, which shows the CBO's estimate of America's debt as a % of GDP, updated with the latest projections from January 2025.

In the past, I have lamented that America's debt problem is caused by its persistently large deficits, which are currently ~6% of GDP and are not expected to close with the current (pre-Trump-2025) set of policies (Figure 9).

One way to close the budget deficit is through spending cuts, which Elon Musk and President Trump are trying to accomplish with DOGE. However, comparing America's federal spending with the rest of the world, at 36.3% of GDP in 2022, the United States is not spending all that much (Figure 10, author's note, the figures from the IMF differ from the CBO as it may include all government spending while CBO is only be considering federal revenues and spending).

In fact, it can be argued that the US government spends far less than its developed country peers, as a % of GDP (this is primarily because the America does not provide universal health care to its citizens, unlike the rest of the developed world). For example, Canada spends 41.4% while Japan spends 44.1% (Figure 11).

On the other hand, the United States also collects far less revenue than its peers (Figure 12). For example, Canada collects 40.6% of GDP compared to America's 32.6%, while France collects 53.5%.

So another way to close America's large deficits may be to raise taxes (yes, I know this is controversial), instead of cutting spending.

Of course, my two simple graphs do not do the topic justice, since taxation and government spending are complicated and nuanced subjects. However, it does beg the question of whether cutting social programs to fund more tax cuts is the proper way to go.

DOGE To Benefit TLTs

The upside risk to TLTs is that DOGE's budget cuts may inadvertently cause a slowdown in U.S. growth, as it trims tens of thousands of government employees and billions of dollars from the federal budget which could be multiplied into hundreds of billions in economic activity while the 'trickle down' benefits of tax cuts are less clear.

For example, laying off tens of thousands of government employees in a short period of time could spike unemployment and reduce consumer spending, as government employees fear for their job safety and hunker down. Similarly, cutting a defense contract may have a ripple effect through production supply chains.

Slower growth should lead to lower long-term treasury yields and higher TLT prices, all things considered. This should benefit the TLTW ETF, although if yields decline rapidly, we should expect TLTW's gains to be capped.

But Beware Black Swans

However, investors should also be mindful that applying Silicon Valley's playbook of 'move fast and break things' to the federal government may destabilize the world's view of US treasuries as safe-haven assets. For example, President Trump recently made an offhand comment implying the U.S. government could selectively default on certain obligations (Figure 13)

Furthermore, Stephen Miran, President Trump's nominee for chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers, appears to be a supporter of the 'Mar-A-Lago Accord' proposal, whereby the United States will use 'guns and tariffs' to force allies to swap their treasuries for zero-coupon century bonds, effectively asking countries to pay for protection and access to American markets (Figure 14).

Selective defaults and forced debt swaps could sour the relations between America and its allies. If that happens, U.S. treasuries could lose its safe-haven status and bond yields may surge, as global investors reprice default risk and exit America's sphere of influence.

While these ideas still appear far-fetched, the fact they are even discussed should raise alarm bells for bond investors.

Conclusion

With President Trump and DOGE working hard to reduce the federal budget, I believe U.S. growth may slow in the coming quarters and lead to lower treasury bond yields. This should be beneficial to the TLTW ETF.

However, the TLTW's gains are capped by its sold call options, so it may not be the best bet for investors looking for lower bond yields. Furthermore, there is a rising risk that President Trump may go too far and turn the world against America, leading to a surge in bond yields. Balancing the risk and potential returns, I continue to view TLTW as a hold.

data.title: American Century High Income Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

High-yield bonds advanced. The U.S. high-yield market rose during the fourth quarter. The election of President-elect Donald Trump was a key driver of market activity in November, with the market generally viewing Trump’s victory and the Republican sweep as positive for risk assets.

Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates, altered its outlook. The Fed followed up its 0.5% interest rate cut in September with two 0.25% cuts in the fourth quarter. This pushed the Fed’s rate target range to 4.25% to 4.5%. But amid persistent inflation and stable economic growth, Fed officials adopted a more cautious stance, boosting their inflation and growth forecasts and reducing their rate cut outlook.

Treasury yields rose. Despite a brief postelection rally, Treasury yields ended the quarter higher, and the yield curve between two- and 10-year bonds steepened. Above-target inflation, mounting U.S. debt and changing investor expectations regarding Fed policy contributed to the yield backdrop, in our view. The yield on the 10-year note climbed 0.79% to 4.58%, while the two-year Treasury yield increased 0.6% to 4.25%.

Inflation persisted. Year-over-year headline inflation, including food and energy prices, increased for the second straight month in November to 2.7%, while core prices remained at 3.3% for the third-consecutive month. Similarly, annual core personal consumption expenditures, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, rose to 2.8% in October and remained there in November.

Spreads tightened. A December sell-off in the Treasury market led to modest spread widening for the month. However, for the quarter overall, high-yield spreads tightened modestly. High-yield bonds significantly outperformed investment-grade securities for the quarter.

Portfolio Performance Review

Lower-quality positioning detracted. Security selection in the CCC ratings category of highly speculative bonds and a slight underweight in higher-rated B-grade bonds relative to the benchmark detracted from results. Although we modestly increased exposure to what we thought were riskier credit opportunities, the portfolio’s weighting in CCC-rated bonds remained close to the benchmark. We also significantly trimmed exposure to investment-grade bonds.

Health facility, packaging company weighed on results. Hospital operator Community Health Systems detracted from results amid uncertainties around Medicaid funding following the election, in our view. An underweight in packaging company Ardagh Group relative to the benchmark also detracted. The company experienced a credit downgrade as its glass business struggled to stabilize volumes.

Chemical, airline bonds contributed. A position in plastics provider Trinseo aided results, as the company announced a transaction that we think should enhance its cash flow profile, extend maturities and foster a cyclical rebound. Underweighting Spirit Airlines relative to the benchmark helped, as the discount airline struggled with lower-than- expected nonticket revenue and reduced its revenue and margin guidance before defaulting in November.

Portfolio Positioning

Our outlook remains upbeat. We believe resilient core economic drivers underpinned by continued earnings growth, supportive data and attractive yields have created a favorable outlook for the high-yield asset class. We remain hopeful that growth will persist, which we think should continue to support high-yield securities.

Trump policies remain unclear. We believe the Trump administration’s large appetite for change, including aggressive proposals for taxes, deregulation, immigration, tariffs and geopolitics, will be a major market driver. While Trump can undertake some executive actions, the Republican Party’s slim Congressional majority leaves some uncertainty about what policies will prevail, in our view.

Regulatory backdrop, trade policy are key. Deregulation, 2017 tax cut extensions and additional taxpayer relief should provide a supportive backdrop for stronger growth and higher profits, in our view. We believe fewer regulations should also create a more favorable environment for mergers and acquisitions, supporting asset prices. Conversely, we think protectionist trade policy and amplified diplomatic tensions could hinder growth and lead to higher inflation.

Goal and Strategy

Seeks to generate attractive income and capital appreciation over time.

Portfolio Management Team

Steve Kotsen, CFA

The letter ratings indicate the credit worthiness of the underlying bonds in the portfolio and generally range from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Generally, as interest rates rise, the value of the securities held in the fund will decline. The opposite is true when interest rates decline.

The lower rated securities in which the fund invests are subject to greater credit risk, default risk and liquidity risk.

Credit risk is the risk that an obligation won’t be paid and a loss will result. Generally, a lower credit rating indicates a greater risk of non-payment. Liquidity risk is the risk that the fund will have difficulty selling its debt securities.

data.title: Visteon Corporation 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Visteon Corporation in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Cool Company: Cheap For Good Reasons, Dividend Still At Risk. Avoid
data.description:
Investment thesis

Cool Company Ltd. (NYSE:CLCO) could face financial pressure due to an increase in spot exposure, low spot rates and a heavy drydock year. This could force the company to further reduce its dividend during the year, as it could start to burn cash starting with Q2 reporting.

With current rates, EPS could become negative in Q4 2025.

Company Background

Cool Company Ltd is a LNGC company, specializing in the transportation of LNG. CoolCo's fleet consists of 12 on the water vessels and one 2025 newbuild.

The company was created as a spin-off from Golar LNG (GLNG), which was a way to divest eight of its Tri-Fuel Diesel Electric (TFDE) vessels. Eastern Pacific invested $150 million to become the main shareholder, which currently owns 58% of outstanding shares.

In the LNGC sector, the type of vessel is very important, since they have very different consumption. Current CoolCo's on the water fleet has an average age of around 8.5 years, with nine TFDE vessels (4x2014 vessels and 5x2015 vessels), two 2020/2021 X-DF vessels and one 2024 ME-GA engine vessel that is the most efficient one. The other 2025 newbuild has the same specifications that the 2024 one.

CoolCo used to have its vessels on long-term charters; however, given recent rate weakness, it has one vessel on the spot market, two more will finish their employment during 2025, and one of the newbuilds doesn't have any contract attached. This could be an important drain on profitability and pose some significant risks.

For the older but still common vessel with tri-fuel diesel engines carrying 160,000 cubic meters of LNG, Atlantic rates were negative for the past week, hitting a record low of minus $2,750 a day on Monday before paring losses to reach minus $1,000 a day on Tuesday, said Spark Commodities analyst Qasim Afghan.

TFDE spot rates are already negative, and 1 year charters are around $20k per day, while CoolCo's backlog is at $85k per day. Just to remember, nine of the fourteen CoolCo vessels are TFDE. Here is a graph with the rates' evolution:

Given this negative rate evolution and outlook, in the Q3 report, the company announced a dividend reduction from the previous $0.41 per share to $0.15 and a new $40 million share repurchase program.

However, if rates don't recover in the next few quarters, the situation could get worse.

LNGC orderbook is one of the biggest in shipping, around 50% of the current fleet, and most of it will be delivered in the next two years. While new liquefaction capacity isn't expected until later in the decade and will probably face delays or never be completed. At least until 2027, or probably later, the market won't be in equilibrium. This implies rates will continue to face pressure for some years, scrapping should be needed, but it seems companies are favoring lay ups, increasing the pain in the long run.

To further increase rate pressure, most cargos are going to Europe due to elevated TTF prices. This reduces ton miles, since the route from the US to Europe is much shorter than to Asia. This situation is likely to persist at least all 2025 due to low European gas storage.

Valuation

Cool Company has no debt maturities until 2029 which provides a stable financial position. At the end of Q3, it had $140 million in cash and $1 billion in debt, which will increase to $1.5 billion with the two newbuilds.

However, if current rates persist during 2025, CoolCo will start burning cash in a couple of quarters, worsening the company's situation. Moreover, 2025 will be an especially bad year for CoolCo as it has many drydocks, with the associated off hire days and costs. Management comments on the CC:

Regarding drydocking, all our TFD vessels were initially acquired around 2014-15, resulting in a concentrated drydock cycle over a two-year period. […] So we now have seven drydocks remaining, six in 2025 and one in 2026, impacting free cash flow to equity by some off-hire time and the associated drydock costs.

Moreover, four of those drydocks will undergo LNGe upgrades, resulting in a higher off-hire around 50 days and higher costs. With that in mind, I have calculated my estimates for Q4 results and 2025. For Q4 spot rates, I have assumed $15,000/day rate. Instead, for 2025 I have assumed the following spot rates: $5,000/day for TFDE and $20,000/day for the newbuild. These rates are higher than current levels, and I believe realized rates could be lower.

If my estimates are correct, Q2 results won't cover the dividend, and the situation will get worse as more LNGCs enter the spot market. For Q4, the company could report negative EPS. Keep in mind that 2025 is the highest delivery year, and liquefaction capacity won't increase substantially until later in the decade. 2026 will probably continue to be a loss-making year due to high deliveries.

Despite being cheap, as KD Research demonstrated in his NAV calculation, I wouldn't be long the stock given my rate expectations. If the current rates persist as I expect, asset values will decline and reduce the current NAV, eliminating the discount. Moreover, I expect the company to further reduce its dividend in a couple of quarters.

Risks

The main risk could be an uptick in rates. Given that the huge orderbook will be delivered as planned and liquefaction capacity doesn't increase substantially until later in the decade, there are only two possibilities for rates to increase.

First, a ton mile increase. This could be possible if, instead of Europe, vessels delivered the gas to Asia. However, given the low inventory levels in Europe, this won't happen this year.

Second, mass scrapping. There could be a mass scrapping of the less efficient vessels, the steam turbine ones. This would reduce the supply of vessels and increase rates. In that scenario, CoolCo could continue to be profitable and pay its current dividend.

Another potential risk could be a take private or takeover. If Eastern Pacific, or any other company, is optimistic about the longer-term outlook, they could decide to pay a premium over the current price to acquire the company. As KD Research demonstrated, there is still quite margin to acquire the company below NAV.

Conclusion

Cool Company faces a challenging market that could worsen its financial position. I expect the company to cut its dividend again during this year due to low spot rates, increased exposure to the spot market and high drydock costs.

I don't recommend holding this company, as the situation could further deteriorate, leading to a couple of difficult years ahead. Even the recent price decline doesn't justify owning the stock.

data.title: Brembo: It's Time To Buy The Premium Brake Manufacturer
data.description:
Introduction

Brembo (OTCPK:BRBOF) (OTCPK:BRBOY) is one of the world’s largest developers and manufacturers of braking systems for automotive vehicles. It doesn’t just manufacture the brake discs, but has now also developed entire braking systems that are being installed in cars and motorbikes. The company recently sold its stake in Pirelli, the Italian tire manufacturer, and used the proceeds of the stake sale to acquire Ohlins Racing, a company focusing on suspensions.

Brembo has its primary listing in Italy, where it's trading with BRE as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume is approximately half a million shares, which represents just under 5M EUR per day. The market capitalization is just under 3.1B EUR as there are approximately 318M shares outstanding (after taking the treasury shares into account).

Some weakness, but the results should remain robust

Brembo still has to publish its full-year results, but there already is plenty of data available to have a pretty good idea of what the company will report on.

The most recent detailed update was the 9M 2024 update, and Brembo announced total revenue of 2.93B EUR in those first nine months of the year. This represented an increase of approximately 9M EUR (0.3%) compared to the first nine months of the previous year, while the EBITDA margin remained pretty consistent at 17.1%.

As you can see below, Brembo generated an EBITDA of 501.1M EUR on that revenue, and after deducting the 199M EUR in depreciation and amortization expenses as well as the 26M EUR in net finance expenses, the pre-tax income was approximately 276M EUR, of which 199.9M EUR hit the bottom line.

Approximately 2.7M EUR of the net profit was attributable to the minority interests in Brembo, which means the attributable net profit was approximately 197.2M EUR which works out to 0.62 EUR per share. That’s quite a bit lower than the 0.71 EUR in earnings in 9M 2023, and this is mainly due to the higher D&A expenses while the net interest expenses also increased. The underlying business is nicely humming along, and the profit decrease is mainly caused by higher finance expenses.

Looking at the cash flow result in the first nine months of the year, total operating cash flow was approximately 507M EUR before taxes and 420M EUR after taking taxes and the use of long-term provisions into account. We should also deduct the 22M EUR in lease payments and payments to minority interests, and that results in an adjusted operating cash flow of 398M EUR.

The total capex incurred in the first nine months of the year was approximately 250M EUR, resulting in a net free cash flow result of 148M EUR (and approximately 154M EUR if I’d use the amount of taxes from the income statement vs. the 82M EUR in cash taxes paid). While that sounds disappointing, keep in mind the total capex plus lease payments in the first nine months of last year came in at 271M EUR, although the combined depreciation and amortization expenses were less than 200M EUR.

Although we still have to wait for full-year results, Brembo did publish a very preliminary and basic trading update which included revenue of 3.84B EUR, on which it generated an EBITDA margin of approximately 17.2%, resulting in an EBITDA of 662M EUR.

There was no update on the net debt position but Brembo anticipated to end the year with 300M EUR in net debt, subsequent to the sale of the Pirelli shares but before making the payment to acquire Ohlins (which I will discuss later in this article). Including the impact of the Ohlins acquisition, the debt ratio remains very reasonable at just 1 times EBITDA.

The sale of the stake in Pirelli will fortify the balance sheet

In October, Brembo also announced it planned to sell its entire stake in Pirelli, the Italian tire manufacturer (I discussed Pirelli in a previous article here). Brembo sold all 55.8 million shares at a fixed price of 5.07 EUR per share, for gross proceeds totalling almost 283M EUR.

Just one week later, Brembo announced the acquisition of Ohlins Racing from Tenneco, a company managed by Apollo Global Management. Ohlins is an important manufacturer of premium high-performance suspension technology for motorcycles and cars in the OEM markets as well as in the motorsport industry.

The acquisition comes with two production facilities and two R&D facilities in Sweden and Thailand. Ohlins is a relatively sizable company with an anticipated revenue of $144M in 2024 with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 21-22%. Using the lower end of that guidance, Ohlins generated approximately 28-29M EUR in EBITDA in 2024. The acquisition didn’t come cheap as Brembo paid a total of $405M for the acquisition, and expressed in USD, this represents an EV/EBITDA multiple of 13.5 and that’s relatively high. The $405M price tag also makes this the largest acquisition ever for Brembo.

There definitely will be some synergy benefits but Brembo has not yet provided its expectations for Ohlins. The acquisition closed in the first week of January, and I expect Brembo to provide more details when it reports on its full-year results in the coming weeks.

Investment thesis

Using the 661M EUR in EBITDA and assuming 270M EUR in depreciation and amortization expenses, the EBIT will come in around 391M EUR. Assuming 32M EUR in net interest expenses and applying a 28% tax rate, the net income should have come in around 258M EUR, of which 255M EUR should be attributable to the shareholders of Brembo. This represents an EPS of 0.80 EUR. The reported free cash flow (excluding the impact of the Pirelli sale) will be lower than that, the sustaining free cash flow result will be higher than that considering the anticipated sustaining capex is just around 80-100M EUR per year.

I currently have no position in Brembo but the stock is now getting increasingly attractive. Although Brembo operates in the premium markets, the results remain robust and I like the diversification into the suspension market. While Brembo will lose dividend income from the Pirelli shares, it will be able to compensate for this with the EBITDA that will be generated by Ohlins.

I’m increasingly interested in initiating a long position in Brembo. I may write some out of the money put options to achieve this.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SFL Corporation: Offshore Drilling Exposure Becomes A Headache Again (Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered SFL Corporation Ltd. or “SFL” (NYSE:SFL) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Please note also that SFL is part of our income model at Value Investor's Edge.

Last week, SFL Corporation reported fourth quarter and full year 2024 results largely in line with consensus expectations and declared an unchanged quarterly cash dividend of $0.27 per share, which will be paid on March 28 to shareholders of record as of March 12. As SFL is domiciled in Bermuda, investors won't have to deal with withholding taxes.

As expected, revenues and Adjusted EBITDA decreased on a sequential basis, mostly due to lower contributions from the harsh environment semi-submersible rig Hercules, which finished its contract with Equinor (EQNR) offshore Canada in November and is now sitting idle in Norway.

With limited near-term employment prospects and estimated daily operating expenses of up to $100,000, the rig will negatively impact the company's results for at least the next couple of quarters.

Should Hercules remain idle for the entire year, cash flow is likely to take a major hit. Please note also that SFL will have to shoulder approximately $180 million in debt amortization this year. As a result, operating cash flow adjusted for debt amortization won't be sufficient to cover the company's current dividend payments:

However, following last month's $150 million bond placement, SFL has more than sufficient liquidity to keep the dividend at current levels well into next year.

On the conference call, management was reluctant to comment on the dividend in a more specific manner but admitted to the issues resulting from the lack of employment for Hercules.

The dividend is set on a quarter-over-quarter basis. So we cannot guide specifically what will be next quarter dividend.

But generally, I would say that the dividend discussions in the company and with the Board is more -- has more to do with the long-term prospects.

And if you look at the company's operating model, yes, we have this legacy asset. And frankly, I mean, the reason why we have this asset in the operating mode where it is, is because it was on a bareboat charter and our counter party, Seadrill failed. They went through two Chapter 11s and we ended up taking the rig back because that was the best for the company at the time.

For my part, I was surprised to hear that Hercules is currently upgraded at Westcon Yards just two years after SFL invested approximately $120 million into the asset.

Should the unit re-enter the Norwegian market, additional investments will be required.

On a more positive note, former related party Seadrill (SDRL) was ordered to pay SFL approximately $48 million in compensation as a result of its breach of contract upon redelivering the rig to SFL in 2022.

However, I would expect Seadrill to appeal the ruling in the coming weeks. In addition, Seadrill appears to have filed a lawsuit against the company:

"in connection with certain capital spares delivered by Seadrill to a subsidiary of SFL in connection with the special survey of Hercules in 2023 where the parties disagree on the actual ownership and compensation of these spares."

The case is due to commence later this year. For my part, I would not be surprised about a comprehensive settlement of both cases at some point going forward.

Without a long-term contract, Hercules will continue to impact SFL's otherwise very stable business model quite meaningfully.

At least in my opinion, the company should approach rig manager Odfjell Drilling (OTCPK:ODFJF) regarding a potential sale. Even in the current market environment, the rig should fetch between $200 million and $250 million in sales proceeds, particularly given Odfjell Drilling's positive assessment of the Norwegian market going forward:

At least in my opinion, the company's traditional income-oriented investor base would likely breathe a sigh of relief upon news of a Hercules sale.

Recently, analysts have cut their estimates for 2025 and 2026 quite meaningfully, with SFL now projected to report a loss this year relative to expectations for earnings per share of $0.94 just five months ago.

With the company's financial performance and dividend sustainability highly dependent on a single non-core asset, I would urge management to consider strategic alternatives for the Hercules sooner rather than later.

Should the rig fail to secure employment going into 2026, I would expect the dividend to be reduced by approximately 30% from current levels.

At this point, SFL's shares are basically a bet on Hercules either being sold at a reasonable price or securing employment in the not-too distant future.

Given the increased likelihood of the rig sitting idle for the entire year and the early part of 2026, I am lowering my rating on SFL Corporation's shares from “Buy” to “Hold.”

However, I do not expect the company to reduce its quarterly cash dividend before the end of the year at the earliest point.

Bottom Line

SFL Corporation reported fourth quarter and full year 2024 results largely in line with expectations and declared an unchanged quarterly cash dividend of $0.27 per share.

However, the company will likely be required to lower the dividend quite meaningfully should the harsh environment rig Hercules fail to secure new work going into 2026.

With the company's financial performance and dividend sustainability highly dependent on a single non-core asset, I would urge management to consider strategic alternatives for the Hercules sooner rather than later.

Given the increased likelihood of the rig sitting idle for the entire year and the early part of 2026, I am lowering my rating on SFL Corporation's shares from “Buy” to “Hold.”

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
